items.title: Storytel Launches AI Genie Software
items.excerpt: Audiobook platform Storytel has launched AI Genie, a conversational companion feature built on Google Gemini, OpenAI, and Anthropic models combined with Storytel's proprietary metadata layer, offering spoiler-free chapter recaps, interactive chats, and hyper-personalized Top Picks reading recommendations. The launch marks a significant shift in how audio subscription platforms are embedding generative AI directly into the listener workflow.

items.title: Children's Authors and Illustrators Launch 'We Are Better Than This' Campaign Against AI
items.excerpt: A coalition of children's book creators including Chris Haughton, Benji Davies, Momoko Abe, Ged Adamson, and Simona Ciraolo launched 'We Are Better Than This,' a film-based creative uprising against AI scraping of illustrators' work, voiced by Miriam Margolyes. The campaign directly challenges AI image-generation pipelines that ingest children's book illustration styles without consent or compensation.

items.title: McGraw Hill Posts Small Profit on Flat Sales in Fiscal 2026, Citing 7.5 Million AI Personalized Learning Users
items.excerpt: McGraw Hill reported that its AI-powered personalized learning tools now serve 7.5 million users, with an agentic AI version of its precision education model in active development, as the company repositions its core revenue around adaptive AI courseware rather than static textbooks. The results signal that AI-native educational publishing is reaching mainstream scale, reshaping how institutions license academic content.

items.title: Beijing International Book Fair Highlights Digital Publishing, Cross-Media Adaptations, and AI Editorial Workflows
items.excerpt: At its 40th-anniversary edition (June 17-21), the Beijing International Book Fair made AI editorial workflows and digital publishing its central theme, with Tencent, NetEase, and Douyin anchoring a new Digital Publishing Joint Pavilion as 1,700 exhibitors from 82 countries attended. Chinese publishers are embedding AI across the entire editorial pipeline—from manuscript evaluation and metadata tagging to web-literature distribution—signaling how the world's largest book market is restructuring around AI.

items.title: Ask the Fellows: Insights from SSP's 2026 Annual Meeting on AI and Research Integrity
items.excerpt: A Scholarly Kitchen Fellow from Ghana's CSIR-Crops Research Institute reported from SSP 2026 on sessions exploring AI in scholarly publishing, the redefinition of research integrity when AI mediates knowledge, and a keynote on global disparities in AI innovation access. The post surfaces how academic publishers are actively debating which parts of the editorial and peer-review workflow can be handed to AI tools versus which require human judgment.

items.title: Legitimate but Not Loved: What Academic Librarians Think About AI
items.excerpt: A global study from Academic Publishing in Europe 2026 finds that librarians accept AI as a legitimate research tool but are deeply uneasy about its implications for trust and research integrity, with the next debate centering on who becomes the trusted authority when AI sits between researchers and the scholarly record. The findings carry direct implications for how publishers design AI features in academic content platforms.

items.title: The Damage Is Done
items.excerpt: Book Riot's newsletter explores the fallout from the Granta/Commonwealth Foundation Short Story Prize AI-use scandal—even after writers were cleared—arguing that the damage to literary publishing trust has already occurred and that editors, prize committees, and platforms must now build new systems for distinguishing human literary work from AI-generated content. The piece frames the episode as a turning point requiring new AI-authenticity infrastructure for literary institutions.

items.title: Around the Book World: Monday, June 22, 2026
items.excerpt: Publishing Perspectives' weekly roundup covers Storytel's Genie AI companion launch, McGraw Hill's 7.5 million AI-learning users, and the inaugural BookTech Collective conference on AI and digital transformation in publishing, aggregating a week of concrete AI-tool deployments across the global book industry. The roundup signals measurable user counts and new conference infrastructure emerging around AI in publishing.

items.title: 15 Best AI Writing Tools for Authors (Updated June 2026): What Each Is Best For
items.excerpt: Kindlepreneur's hands-tested guide to AI writing tools for authors was updated in June 2026, covering Sudowrite, Novelcrafter, Dibbly Create, and newer entrants in the fiction and nonfiction AI-drafting space, with honest assessments of which tools handle long-form narrative versus marketing copy versus developmental editing. The update reflects a maturing AI-tools ecosystem where authors can now choose purpose-built fiction models rather than adapting general-purpose LLMs.

items.title: Novelcrafter Review (Updated June 2026): Is It the Most Powerful AI Tool for Writers?
items.excerpt: Kindlepreneur's June 2026 update to its Novelcrafter review evaluates the platform's Codex world-management system, Bring Your Own Key AI model architecture, and updated pricing tiers, positioning it as the most powerful dedicated AI environment for fiction authors doing serious long-form work. The review reflects how fiction-specific AI tools have diverged from general-purpose LLMs, with authors choosing purpose-built ecosystems that preserve narrative consistency and creative voice.

items.title: Links of Interest: June 24, 2026 — Bookpact.ai, Anthropic Suit Opt-Outs, UK AI Licensing Roundup
items.excerpt: Jane Friedman's June 24 links column covers Dutch publishers launching Bookpact.ai, a consent-based AI licensing platform; authors who opted out of the Anthropic settlement filing for up to $150,000 per work in statutory damages; and the UK Publishers' Licensing Services reaching 250 opted-in publishers for its collective AI licensing scheme. The items trace the emerging commercial and legal infrastructure authors and publishers are building to manage—and monetize—their relationships with AI companies.

items.title: Links of Interest: June 17, 2026 — Kobo's AI Dilemma, Google Play AI Book Insights, Author Lawsuit Ruling
items.excerpt: Jane Friedman's June 17 links column highlights Kobo CEO Michael Tamblyn wrestling publicly with how much AI content is too much on its self-publishing platform, Google Play launching AI-powered book insights with publisher opt-out, and a US judge ruling that authors suing AI companies must pursue each company separately. The three items together show how platform gatekeepers, AI developers, and legal systems are each independently shaping the rules of AI in publishing.

items.title: Bestselling Nonfiction Author Believes AI Has Devastated His Book Sales
items.excerpt: A bestselling nonfiction author reports that AI-powered search and answer engines have begun replacing the informational role of how-to and self-help books, with 2025 marking the first steep sales drop and 2026 tracking worse, projecting an 80% reduction in copies sold versus 2022. The case study signals that AI is now directly competing with books-as-information-delivery, representing a new structural threat beyond AI-generated content flooding the market.
